perturbation.original_text: The PA MHC and Federal MHC are hereinafter referred to collectively as the "MHCs." Pursuant to the Plans, the Company will offer to certain depositors of the Bank and to the Bank's tax qualified employee benefit plans, including the Bank's employee stock ownership plan (the "ESOP") (collectively, the "Employee Plans"), rights to subscribe for the Securities in a subscription offering (the "Subscription Offering").
perturbation.changed_text: The PA MHC and Federal MHC are hereinafter referred to collectively as the "MHCs." Pursuant to the Plans, the Company will offer to select individuals at its sole discretion and to the Bank's tax qualified employee benefit plans, excluding the Bank's employee stock ownership plan (the "ESOP") (collectively, the "Employee Plans"), rights to subscribe for the Securities in a subscription offering (the "Subscription Offering").
perturbation.explanation: By limiting subscription rights to select individuals at the company's discretion, and by excluding the ESOP plan, the modified text contradicts established requirements for fair and equal opportunity in subscription offerings for all depositors under federal banking regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 CFR § 192.300 states that "In a subscription offering, an eligible account holder as defined in § 192.290(b) will receive, without payment, nontransferable rights to purchase capital stock of the converted savings association" and "an eligible account holder will receive subscription rights to purchase the capital stock prior to any offering to the general public.". The modified text excludes depositors and the ESOP from mandatory subscription rights, thereby contradicting this regulation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 CFR § 192.300 - Offering circular requirements. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 12 CFR § 192.300 - Offering circular requirements. CFR prev | next § 192.300 Offering circular requirements. (a) Content and filing requirements. A savings association must prepare and file its offering circular in compliance with this part, Form OC, and the applicable SEC registration statement form required under 12 CFR 16.15 . A Federal savings association must file its offering circular with the appropriate OCC licensing office and a State savings association must file its offering circular with the appropriate FDIC region. If filing an amendment, the savings association also must comply with §§ 192.155 and 192.165. (b) Member approval. A savings association must condition its stock offering upon member approval of its plan of conversion. (c) Agency review. The appropriate Federal banking agency will review the offering circular and may comment on the included disclosures and financial statements. The appropriate Federal banking agency will not approve the adequacy or accuracy of the offering circular or the disclosures. (d) Revised filings. A savings association must file any revised offering circular , final offering circular , and any post-effective amendment to the final offering circular in accordance with the procedures in §§ 192.155 and 192.165. (e) Request for effectiveness. After a savings association satisfactorily addresses the appropriate Federal banking agency 's comments, the savings association must request that the appropriate Federal banking agency declare the offering circular effective for a time period. The time period may not exceed the maximum time period for the completion of the sale of all of the savings association 's shares under § 192.400 . CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 12 CFR § 192.300 by limiting subscription rights to select individuals and excluding the ESOP, while the law explanation states that this regulation ensures eligible account holders receive nontransferable rights to purchase capital stock prior to any offering to the general public. The original text ensured these rights for all depositors and employee plans, including the ESOP, as implied by 'Pursuant to the Plans, the Company will offer to certain depositors of the Bank and to the Bank's tax qualified employee benefit plans, including the Bank's employee stock ownership plan (the "ESOP") (collectively, the "Employee Plans").' The scraped snippets confirm the existence of this regulation (12 CFR § 192.300) and its relevance to offering circular requirements, supporting the assessment that the modification constitutes a direct contradiction by denying subscription rights to eligible parties.

perturbation.original_text: The Bank is a member in good standing of the Federal Home Loan Bank of Pittsburgh; the deposit accounts of the Bank are insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits. The Bank is a "qualified thrift lender" within the meaning of 12 U.S.C. Section 1467a(m).
perturbation.changed_text: The Bank is a member in good standing of the Federal Home Loan Bank of Pittsburgh; the deposit accounts of the Bank may or may not be insured by the FDIC at its discretion. The Bank strives to be a "qualified thrift lender" within the aspirational meaning of 12 U.S.C. Section 1467a(m).
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces uncertainty regarding FDIC insurance and the bank's status as a qualified thrift lender, directly contradicting legal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 U.S.C. § 1811 establishes the FDIC and mandates deposit insurance for eligible institutions, while also implying the bank is actively striving to meet the qualified thrift lender status, not mandating the status. The modification suggests deposit insurance is discretionary, contradicting the mandatory nature of FDIC insurance, and that the QTL status is something the bank will work toward - without mandating their compliance.

Cambiar a español Search FDIC.gov Search Deposit Insurance Understanding Deposit Insurance Share This: Share on Facebook Share on X Share on X Follow the FDIC on LinkedIn Share through email Print Deposit Insurance Understanding Deposit Insurance Deposit Insurance FAQs Are My Deposit Accounts Insured by the FDIC? Financial Products That Are Not Insured by the FDIC Deposit Insurance for Accounts Held by Government Depositors Brochures Deposit Insurance At A Glance Your Insured Deposits Financial Institution Employee’s Guide to Deposit Insurance Questions and Answers Related to the FDIC’s Part 328 Final Rule Deposit Insurance Fund Assessment Methodology & Rates Assessment Calculators Fund Management Historical Information Deposit Insurance Fund Background Materials Congressionally Required Studies Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 Federal Deposit Insurance Reform Act of 2005 Historical Designated Reserve Ratio Assessment Regulations Deposit Insurance Fund Contact Information Vea esta página en español FDIC deposit insurance protects your money in deposit accounts at FDIC-insured banks in the event of a bank failure. Since the FDIC was founded in 1933, no depositor has lost a penny of FDIC-insured funds. How FDIC Deposit Insurance Works The FDIC helps maintain stability and public confidence in the U.S. financial system. One way we do this is by insuring deposits to at least $250,000 per depositor, per ownership category at each FDIC-insured bank. The FDIC maintains the Deposit Insurance Fund (DIF), which: Insures deposits and protects depositors of FDIC-insured banks and Helps fund our resolution activities when banks fail. The DIF is backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government, and it has two sources of funds: Assessments (insurance premiums) that FDIC-insured institutions pay and Interest earned on funds invested in U.S. government obligations. The FDIC buys Treasury notes, and the interest on those notes helps the DIF grow. FDIC deposit insurance only covers deposits, and only if your bank is FDIC-insured. Make sure your bank is FDIC-insured, using the BankFind Suite search tool . How to Know If Your Account is Covered FDIC insurance covers deposits in all types of accounts at FDIC-insured banks, but it does not cover non-deposit investment products, even those offered by FDIC-insured banks. Additionally, FDIC deposit insurance doesn’t cover default or bankruptcy of any non-FDIC-insured institution. Covered Money deposited at FDIC-insured banks in: Checking accounts Negotiable order of withdrawal (NOW) accounts Savings accounts Money market deposit accounts (MMDAs) Time deposits such as certificates of deposit (CDs) Cashier’s checks, money orders, and other official items issued by a bank Not Covered Stock investments Bond investments Mutual funds Annuities Life insurance policies Safe deposit boxes or their contents U.S. Treasury bills, bonds, or notes Municipal securities Crypto assets Understanding Your Coverage Limits FDIC deposit insurance covers $250,000 per depositor, per FDIC-insured bank, for each account ownership category . Ownership categories include: Single Accounts Joint Accounts Certain Retirement Accounts —for example, Individual Retirement Accounts (IRAs) Trust Accounts Employee Benefit Plan Accounts Corporation / Partnership / Unincorporated Association Accounts Government Accounts All of your deposits in the same ownership category in the same FDIC-insured bank are added together for the purpose of determinin
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces uncertainty about FDIC insurance and qualified thrift lender status. The original text states the bank's deposit accounts 'are insured by the FDIC,' while the changed text states the accounts 'may or may not be insured by the FDIC at its discretion.' This contradicts the purpose of FDIC insurance to protect depositors, as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'FDIC deposit insurance protects your money in deposit accounts at FDIC-insured banks in the event of a bank failure.' The modification directly opposes the mandatory insurance provided by the FDIC and introduces an element of discretion that undermines the legal requirement. The 'qualified thrift lender' status is also weakened, impacting the bank's legal standing.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will pay any stock issue and transfer taxes which may be payable with respect to the sale of the Securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may choose to pay any stock issue and transfer taxes, subject to its discretion, with respect to the sale of the Securities.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text makes the payment of stock issue and transfer taxes discretionary, contradicting the legal obligation to pay such taxes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 4301 - Tax on transfers of stock and certificates of indebtedness
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 4301 imposes a tax on transfers of stock. The modified text makes the payment optional, which is a direct contradiction of the law. While this section may be out of date, there still remains legal precedent on stock transfer taxes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that 'The Company will pay' taxes related to the sale of securities, creating an obligation. The changed text says 'The Company may choose to pay,' making the payment discretionary. The perturbation explanation states that this change contradicts the legal obligation to pay such taxes, and the Law Explanation, though citing a potentially outdated code, reinforces the existence of legal precedent on stock transfer taxes. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the core contradiction lies in replacing a mandatory action ('will pay') with a discretionary one ('may choose to pay') regarding legally required taxes, fulfilling the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction'.
